prompt: Write a paper on Middle Eastern and North African Films with Transgender Themes. Provide a broad overview with extensive references to both trans theory and film theory, and make sure to include in-depth discussion of at least three films.
article:
### 1) Scope, terms, and why “trans-themed MENA cinema” is formally and politically distinctive

**Scope (assumption stated):** “MENA” here includes Arab-majority states plus **Iran, Turkey, and Israel/Palestine**, reflecting how most trans-themed films from the region circulate through transnational festival and funding networks. “Transgender themes” includes: (a) films with explicitly trans protagonists or major trans characters; (b) films centered on transition, legal/medical recognition, or trans community life; and (c) films where **gender legibility/passing, administrative classification, and/or embodied voice** are the explicit dramatic motors—even if local labels (e.g., “transsexual,” “sex change,” “cross-dressing,” “travesti”) differ.

A major through-line across MENA cinemas is that “transness” often becomes **cinematically narratable** at the precise point where it intersects with **institutions**—courts, clinics, identity documents, policing, border regimes, censorship boards, and the moral economies of family and neighborhood. This is not incidental: trans studies highlights how sex/gender categories are **produced and enforced** through medicalization and state classification (IDs, passports, birth certificates), making some bodies intelligible and others precarious or “unlivable” [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php). In MENA contexts, this institutionalization is often intensified by (i) **religious-legal discourses** about permissibility, (ii) **morality policing and censorship** regimes, and (iii) **uneven access to legal gender recognition** (often judge-driven, expensive, and contingent) [[94]](https://www.hrw.org/report/2019/09/03/dont-punish-me-who-i-am/systemic-discrimination-against-transgender-women-lebanon), [[101]](https://www.hrw.org/news/2022/04/08/tough-territory-transgender-people-middle-east-and-north-africa).

---

### 2) Political–legal–media ecology shaping what can be filmed (and how)

#### 2.1 Iran: permissibility of surgery + punitive sexuality regimes + censorship → “administrative melodrama”
Iran is frequently described (including in Iranian public-health literature) as a place where sex/gender reassignment has a distinctive **jurisprudential legitimacy** in Shia contexts: Khomeini is reported to have issued fatwas legitimizing reassignment/confirmation (1964; 1982) [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9745420/). A medical/public-health account also links this to **administrative consequences** (IDs, inheritance rules, court processes) and to a state-linked loan mechanism via the Imam Committee [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9745420/). Simultaneously, that same source underscores discrimination, ridicule, honor violence risks, and non-alignment with international standards of care [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9745420/).

For cinema, Iran’s **Ministry of Culture and Islamic Guidance** licensing regime and a broader ecology of conservative backlash shape representability. A peer-reviewed article argues that censorship contributes to the prominence of **cross-dressing/passing figures** in New Iranian Cinema, noting that filmmakers have used such tropes to sidestep restrictions; it also documents non-state actor violence against theaters over a cross-dressing-centered film and interprets these conflicts as opening “queer and transgender potential” in public space [[88]](https://transreads.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/2021-12-23_61c4e7e24d00f_Cross-DressingandGenderTrespassing-TheTransgenderMoveasaSiteofAgentialPotentialintheNewIranianCinema.pdf). IranWire likewise describes systematic licensing pressure and self-censorship tied to the Ministry’s screening/broadcast permissions [[89]](https://iranwire.com/en/features/70723/).

**Implication for film form:** Iranian trans-themed cinema often becomes a cinema of **documents and thresholds**: passports, court approvals, clinic permissions—cinematic objects that dramatize what Dean Spade calls the harms and sorting power of administrative systems (“administrative violence”) even when reforms are framed as “recognition” [[26]](https://www.deanspade.net/books/normal-life/).

#### 2.2 Turkey: medicalized legal recognition + contemporary exhibition bans → the politics of visibility and circulation
Amnesty International documents Turkey’s Civil Code Article 40 requirements for “changing gender,” including psychiatric diagnosis and (historically) permanent incapacity of reproduction, and reports that the European Court of Human Rights found the sterilization precondition to access gender reassignment surgery violated Article 8 in **Y.Y. v. Turkey** [[91]](https://www.amnesty.org/es/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/EUR4412052015ENGLISH.pdf). This is a paradigmatic case where trans “recognition” is conditioned by medicalized gatekeeping.

Exhibition contexts have also tightened. In 2024, press reporting described a local authority ban on a queer film screening in Istanbul on grounds of public security/peace, prompting the organizer (Mubi) to cancel an entire festival [[92]](https://www.voanews.com/a/7857598.html), [[93]](https://www.theguardian.com/film/2024/nov/07/turkish-film-festival-daniel-craig-queer-censorship). This climate matters for trans-themed films’ domestic reach, and it helps explain why some Turkish trans documentaries build their publics largely via **international festivals**, diaspora circuits, and streaming rather than national theatrical release (see *Blue ID* below) [[6]](https://publications.idfa.nl/idfa-bertha-fund-activity-report-2023/in-the-spotlight-burcu-melekoglu-and-vuslat-karan-blue-id).

#### 2.3 Lebanon: General Security pre-censorship + policing of “morality” + precarious legal gender recognition
Human Rights Watch describes Lebanese trans women facing systemic discrimination and elevated risks of arbitrary arrest and violence, with enforcement patterns tied to penal code provisions and morality/sex work laws; HRW also emphasizes the vulnerability created by ID documents not matching gender expression [[94]](https://www.hrw.org/report/2019/09/03/dont-punish-me-who-i-am/systemic-discrimination-against-transgender-women-lebanon). HRW documents that changing name/gender markers requires a court ruling and notes a 2016 appeals-court ruling allowing a transgender man to change markers without surgery being a prerequisite—while also detailing barriers like cost, prolonged proceedings, and limited legal support [[94]](https://www.hrw.org/report/2019/09/03/dont-punish-me-who-i-am/systemic-discrimination-against-transgender-women-lebanon).

Lebanon also has a long-standing **formal prior-censorship** apparatus: a legal/practice report describes General Security authority over cinema via laws dating to 1934 and 1947 that require licensing and subject “cinematic tapes” to censorship; it notes practices like cutting scenes/terms or prohibiting production and cites confiscations of films depicting homosexual relationships as “natural” [[95]](https://lb.boell.org/sites/default/files/2010._censorship_in_lebanon-_law_practice_en.pdf). Even when trans lives are not named in law, these censorship levers shape what queer/trans stories can be publicly screened.

#### 2.4 Tunisia / North Africa: criminalization, pathologization, and festival backlash as a media event
Human-rights documentation indicates Tunisia criminalizes same-sex acts and that courts have treated trans identity as “mental illness” in denying legal gender recognition, requiring “biological medical condition” evidence [[103]](https://www.humandignitytrust.org/country-profile/Tunisia/). A broader HRW regional analysis (Egypt, Lebanon, Tunisia) stresses that many countries lack clear avenues for legal gender recognition, producing judge-driven processes (3–10 years) and incentivizing “completed” medicalized narratives with forensic scrutiny [[101]](https://www.hrw.org/news/2022/04/08/tough-territory-transgender-people-middle-east-and-north-africa).

The **publicness** of queer/trans visibility can become a flashpoint even in film-festival contexts: a Tunisian trans woman (Sandra) received harassment and death threats after appearing on the red carpet in connection with a documentary about LGBTQ lives in Tunisia [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual). This is crucial for understanding documentary ethics: the festival becomes part of the film’s apparatus of exposure.

#### 2.5 Israel/Palestine: administrative easing (reported) + “pinkwashing” conflict in global circulation
Haaretz reports reforms easing gender marker change on IDs without surgery and without requiring starting hormone therapy [[97]](https://www.haaretz.com/israel-news/2020-02-26/ty-article/.premium/israel-approves-allowing-transgender-people-to-change-gender-on-ids-without-surgery/0000017f-e522-df5f-a17f-fffe0c160000). An official Ministry of Foreign Affairs service page (for citizens abroad) describes a procedure requiring physician forms and approvals by the Ministry of Health and Population and Immigration Authority [[98]](https://embassies.gov.il/atlanta/en/services/israeli-citizens/notice-change-gender). At the same time, queer cinema in Israel/Palestine is deeply entangled with “pinkwashing” debates and boycott politics; advocacy initiatives like **Queer Cinema for Palestine** explicitly position queer film exhibition as anti-occupation solidarity and call for refusing participation in certain state-linked festival contexts [[99]](https://queercinemaforpalestine.org/), [[100]](https://queercinemaforpalestine.org/faqs/). For trans-themed films, this means reception is often **overdetermined**: a film can be read simultaneously as trans testimony, as migration story, and as an artifact in geopolitical struggle.

---

### 3) Theoretical backbone: trans studies × film theory (an analytic toolkit)

This section is intentionally dense: the point is to show how **trans theory** and **film theory** jointly explain what these films do, not merely what they depict.

#### 3.1 Trans studies concepts most relevant to MENA film objects

1) **Medicalization and diagnostic sorting**  
Transgender studies centrally examines how sex/gender variance is produced through medical and legal regimes, and how “bona fide” transness is often created through clinical narratives and gatekeeping [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php). This is especially salient for Iran (fatwa + clinic + court) [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9745420/), [[51]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/professing-selves) and Turkey (legal change tied to psychiatric and reproductive criteria) [[91]](https://www.amnesty.org/es/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/EUR4412052015ENGLISH.pdf).

2) **Administrative power and “administrative violence”**  
Spade critiques equality/recognition strategies that seek inclusion in state systems while leaving intact the administrative mechanisms that distribute vulnerability (housing, prisons, IDs, welfare) [[26]](https://www.deanspade.net/books/normal-life/). For trans-themed cinema, this reframes the “ID card” or “passport” not as a neutral plot device but as a **violence-bearing object** that sorts whose life can move, work, or remain safe.

3) **Legibility, passing, and the politics of visibility**  
The Transgender Studies Reader framework emphasizes cultural intelligibility and the material consequences of being rendered unintelligible [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php). In trans cinema, visibility is not a simple good: it can be a condition of recognition *and* a vector of danger.

4) **Trans film temporality and the problem of being “seen as trans”**  
Halberstam’s “The Transgender Look” argues that transgender film is structured by a paradox of **visibility and temporality**, where being seen as trans can mark “failing to pass” and can rupture past/present/future registers; Halberstam also proposes the possibility of a non-fetishistic gaze that “looks with” rather than “at” the trans body [[27]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003206255-23/transgender-look-jack-halberstam). This becomes a powerful formal question for MENA films where disclosure scenes (to family, clinic, employer, or camera) are pivotal.

5) **Narrative, autobiography, and “transition as plot”**  
Prosser’s *Second Skins* frames transsexual autobiography as a constitutive narrative force—“Sex change is a plot”—and positions trans embodiment and narrative as mutually shaping [[28]](https://cup.columbia.edu/book/second-skins/9780231533805/). This is crucial for films where transness is made credible through storytelling to authorities (judges, doctors) or to the audience.

6) **Iran-specific trans studies: “professing selves” and the state’s paradox**  
Najmabadi argues that in Iran, law, psychiatry, jurisprudence, and biomedicine collaborate to distinguish acceptable “true” transsexuality from unacceptable “true” homosexuality, and that regulation paradoxically opens spaces for non-normative living because recognition depends on **the stories people tell**—the selves they profess [[51]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/professing-selves). This is almost a blueprint for reading Iranian trans films: narrative voice is not just expression; it is **a survival technology**.

#### 3.2 Film theory frameworks that illuminate trans-themed MENA cinema

1) **The gaze and visual pleasure**  
Mulvey’s foundational argument about classical cinema organizing looking relations and pleasure through gendered power (often summarized as the “male gaze”) remains a baseline for analyzing how bodies are made spectacle or subject [[105]](https://horrorlex.com/results/visual-pleasure-and-narrative-cinema/Mulvey,%20Laura/686529383692b965e8f9e585). Trans-themed MENA films often complicate this by foregrounding **who is allowed to look**, under what danger, and whether the camera produces objectification or solidarity.

2) **Apparatus theory, ideology, and suture as “making a subject”**  
Baudry’s apparatus account ties cinematic continuity and identification to ideological effects and to producing a unified viewing subject through continuity and identification mechanisms [[118]](https://www.scilit.com/publications/48d25ecea51484edce21dea04a46d1ad). For trans-themed cinema—especially documentaries—this raises the question: does the film “suture” the viewer into a normative interpretive position (e.g., diagnosing, judging, pitying), or does it produce a more unstable identification that resists mastery?

3) **Voice, sound, and gendered normativity**  
Silverman argues that sexual difference is produced by cinema’s sound regime as well as visual regime; the female voice is disciplined and made to function normatively within classic cinema, and voice becomes a site of power and containment [[41]](https://monoskop.org/images/b/b4/Silverman_Kaja_The_Acoustic_Mirror_The_Female_Voice_in_Psychoanalysis_and_Cinema_1988.pdf). For trans films, **voice is often the pivot of legibility** (phone calls, interviews, bureaucratic speech, stage performance, confessional testimony). Silverman helps analyze when voice is extracted (confession) versus self-authored (address).

4) **Documentary “voice,” modes, performance, and ethics**  
Nichols’ documentary studies canon offers vocabulary for documentary “voice” [[108]](https://www.jstor.org/stable/i370747) and for reading modes and ethical centrality [[107]](https://iupress.org/9780253026859/introduction-to-documentary-third-edition/). Bruzzi’s account (as framed on Routledge’s page) emphasizes documentary as performance rather than passive observation [[109]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9780203967386/new-documentary-stella-bruzzi). Renov’s *Subject of Documentary* foregrounds the rise of autobiographical/first-person documentary where filmmakers become subjects and documentary constructs selfhood, often amid oppression/trauma [[111]](https://www.upress.umn.edu/9780816634415/subject-of-documentary/). These frameworks matter directly for MENA trans documentaries where the camera may expose subjects to policing or familial harm (cf. Tunisia backlash) [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual), and where “participation” can become coerced by circumstances.

5) **Melodrama and “body genres” (affect as politics)**  
Williams’ “Film Bodies” helps analyze how genre organizes bodily response and excess; melodrama is a key structure in many trans-themed family narratives (expulsion, illness, return, recognition) [[124]](https://web.colby.edu/sp266/files/2012/09/filmbodies.pdf). In MENA contexts, melodrama is not merely sentiment; it can be a strategy to make taboo social truths publicly speakable.

6) **Embodiment, touch, and intercultural memory**  
Marks’ “haptic visuality” describes images that evoke touch and bodily memory, offering a way to read films that rely on textures, archives, domestic spaces, and embodied traces rather than explicit exposition [[133]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/the-skin-of-the-film). This is especially helpful for archive-driven trans documentaries (Lebanon) [[1]](https://festival.idfa.nl/en/film/a6aa4102-ea72-4f6e-a7f0-21d17dcfd526/treat-me-like-your-mother/) and intimate domestic portraits (Tunisia) [[167]](https://www.facebook.com/filmfestival.gr/posts/tdf19-world-premiereupon-the-shadow-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisia-2017-80-human-ri/1810334835658981/).

---

### 4) A working corpus: notable MENA films with transgender themes (selected filmography with reliable anchors)

This list prioritizes titles with **festival archives, distributor catalogs, or major publication records** in the provided source set.

#### Iran
- **Be Like Others** (*Zenanne*) — Tanaz Eshaghian, 2008, documentary; countries listed as Canada/Iran/UK/USA; 74 min; described as screening at Sundance and Berlinale in one program listing [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others). Film follows candidates navigating psychiatric permission, surgery, and social stigma amid the state distinction between homosexuality and transsexuality [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others).  
- **Facing Mirrors** (*Aynehaye rooberoo*) — Negar Azarbayjani, 2011, narrative; Iran/Germany; 102 min; distribution/festival record via The Film Collaborative; widely framed as the first Iranian narrative film with a trans main character [[14]](https://www.thefilmcollaborative.org/films/facingmirrors). Frameline provides language as Persian & German [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors).

#### Turkey
- **Trans X Istanbul** — Maria Binder, 2014, documentary; world premiere at the 33rd Istanbul Film Festival (official site); focuses on trans women, activism, displacement, hate murders, and Gezi-era politics [[9]](https://www.transxistanbul.com/). Ethnofest lists 109 min, German/Turkish [[78]](https://www.ethnofest.gr/en/film/trans-x-istanbul/).  
- **Blue ID** (*Mavi kimlik*) — Burcu Melekoglu & Vuslat Karan, 2022, documentary; IDFA archive (60 min) about trans actor Rüzgar Erkoçlar and the media storm around his transition; ID card color change is central [[148]](https://www.idfa.nl/en/film/7cd2b58a-edfb-46b8-b986-a8db9a35c3ae/blue-id). IDFA Bertha Fund report: premiered IDFA 2022, won NPO Audience Award; not yet premiered in Turkey at time of reporting; distribution obstacles noted [[6]](https://publications.idfa.nl/idfa-bertha-fund-activity-report-2023/in-the-spotlight-burcu-melekoglu-and-vuslat-karan-blue-id).

#### Lebanon
- **Treat Me Like Your Mother** — Mohamad Abdouni, 2025, documentary; IDFA archive: 76 min, Arabic; world premiere IDFA Envision Competition; built from personal photo archives of four Lebanese trans women and traces Beirut trans life across decades [[1]](https://festival.idfa.nl/en/film/a6aa4102-ea72-4f6e-a7f0-21d17dcfd526/treat-me-like-your-mother/). Lux Film Festival also lists it (Lebanon, 2025, 76 min) [[157]](https://www.luxfilmfest.lu/en/movies/treat-me-like-your-mother/).

#### Israel/Palestine
- **Melting Away** (*Namess Ba’Geshem*) — Doron Eran, 2011 (as listed by Frameline), narrative; 86 min, Hebrew; trans daughter narrative centered on familial expulsion and return [[81]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline37/melting-away). Director interview emphasizes targeting parents and references the 2009 Tel Aviv LGBT youth center shooting as catalyst for making the film [[82]](https://epgn.com/2012/03/29/18042961-warming-up-to-melting-away-israeli-film-festival-to-screen-lgbt-title/).  
- **The Belle from Gaza** (*La Belle de Gaza*) — Yolande Zauberman, Cannes Special Screenings 2024; official Cannes page lists duration 76 min, production year 2023, country France, distribution and sales by Pyramide [[150]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/f/la-belle-de-gaza/). Cannes interview frames a nocturnal quest around Arab trans women on Hatnufa Street and the rumored “Belle” who walked from Gaza to Tel Aviv; subjects’ safety and concealment (hijab/veil) appear [[151]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/2024/interview-with-yolande-zauberman-searching-for-the-legendary-belle-from-gaza/). Pyramide catalog lists languages (English/Arabic/French/Hebrew) and extensive festival screenings [[154]](http://inter.pyramidefilms.com/pyramide-international-catalogue-t/the-belle-from-gaza.html).  
- **Melting Away** also has additional festival/distributor metadata in regional festival archives [[16]](https://desertfilmsociety.org/archives/dfs-screening-tba-27/), but the most controlled citations in this dataset are Frameline and the director interview [[81]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline37/melting-away), [[82]](https://epgn.com/2012/03/29/18042961-warming-up-to-melting-away-israeli-film-festival-to-screen-lgbt-title/).

#### Tunisia / North Africa
- **Upon the Shadow** (*Au-delà de l’ombre / في الظل*) — Nada Mezni Hafaiedh, 2016/2017 (festival vs release-year variance); Thessaloniki Documentary Festival post announces world premiere and lists 80 min, Tunisia, “Human Rights” program; includes a trans participant Sandra [[167]](https://www.facebook.com/filmfestival.gr/posts/tdf19-world-premiereupon-the-shadow-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisia-2017-80-human-ri/1810334835658981/). Apple TV lists 2017, 1h20m, Arabic, Tunisia, and names Sandra Neifer in cast [[166]](https://tv.apple.com/us/movie/upon-the-shadow/umc.cmc.2ph0x2ohl48nf5clbjl8vzia2). Carthage Film Festival official page documents Bronze Tanit award for *Au-delà de l’ombre* [[170]](https://www.facebook.com/JCC.TN/posts/tanit-de-bronze-%C3%A0-au-del%C3%A0-de-lombre-de-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisie-karim-kamoun/1972660296325857/). Raseef22 documents transphobic backlash and death threats directed at Sandra around Carthage red-carpet appearance [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual).

#### Morocco (transgender themes, but often via coercion or “passing” rather than trans selfhood)
- **Woman’s Hearts** — reviewed by Variety as centered on a “pre-op Moroccan transsexual dressmaker” in Italy who returns to Casablanca with a client [[174]](https://variety.com/2008/film/markets-festivals/woman-s-hearts-1200547891/). (In this dataset, reliable institutional festival/distributor metadata is not available; treat as a thematic lead, not a fully verified filmography entry.)

---

### 5) In-depth analyses (four films)

I focus on **four** case studies to exceed the “at least three films” requirement and to show cross-form variety: Iranian narrative, Iranian documentary, Turkish documentary, Tunisian documentary. A shorter comparative section then connects Lebanon and Israel/Palestine works.

---

## 5.1 *Facing Mirrors* (Negar Azarbayjani, 2011, Iran/Germany): the road movie as a machine for trans legibility, class encounter, and administrative time

**Core metadata and positioning:** The Film Collaborative lists *Facing Mirrors* (2011, Iran/Germany, 102 min) and explicitly frames it as “the first narrative film from Iran to feature a transgender main character” [[14]](https://www.thefilmcollaborative.org/films/facingmirrors). Frameline similarly describes it as the first Iranian narrative feature with a transgender protagonist and gives language as Persian & German, with a plot anchored in the attempt to get a passport and go to Germany for surgery [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors). Variety’s review emphasizes the taxi-ride structure and frames the relationship not as liberal “tolerance” but solidarity across difference [[65]](https://variety.com/2011/film/reviews/facing-mirrors-1117945989/).

### (a) Transition is not “an inner truth revealed” but an **administrative sequence**
The inciting problem is **documentation and mobility**: Eddie/Adineh is waiting for a passport to leave Iran for surgery in Germany [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors). This is the cinematic form of what trans studies frames as the power of classification systems (IDs, passports) in producing legibility and distributing vulnerability [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php). Eddie’s life is literally suspended in **bureaucratic time**—a waiting-room temporality that Halberstam’s trans film temporality helps theorize: the future (post-surgery, post-passing, post-border crossing) exerts pressure on the present, while the past (assigned gender, family name) returns as threat [[27]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003206255-23/transgender-look-jack-halberstam).

### (b) Disclosure, panic, and the scene of “being seen”
Frameline’s synopsis describes Eddie trying to explain being “trans,” which triggers Rana’s panic and a near-fatal car crash [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors). This is a key cinematic condensation of **visibility-as-danger**: being seen and named is not liberation but a rupture that threatens catastrophe. Halberstam’s formulation—when the trans character is seen as trans, passing fails and time registers rupture—maps cleanly onto this crash as a literal break in continuity [[27]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003206255-23/transgender-look-jack-halberstam). The crash is also an apparatus-level event: it interrupts narrative flow and forces a re-suturing of viewer identification. Do we align with Rana’s fear? With Eddie’s danger? The film’s ethical achievement (as described by Variety) is pushing beyond “tolerance” to solidarity [[65]](https://variety.com/2011/film/reviews/facing-mirrors-1117945989/).

### (c) Class, gender, and the “taboo” woman driver: transness mirrored
Rana is described as a conservative wife/mother driving a cab clandestinely to pay off debts and keep her husband out of prison [[14]](https://www.thefilmcollaborative.org/films/facingmirrors). Frameline notes that a woman driving a cab is itself taboo in Rana’s class milieu [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors). The title’s “mirrors” are not merely identity metaphors; they are **social** mirrors: Eddie’s transgression of gender assignment and Rana’s transgression of gendered labor norms produce a shared condition of social risk. Trans studies’ insistence on intersectional distribution of vulnerability—how harms are mediated by class, gender expression, and institutional exposure—clarifies why solidarity becomes possible: each woman’s “deviance” is policed differently but structurally [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php).

### (d) Melodrama without moralism
While we do not have a dedicated melodrama-theory excerpt about this film specifically, Williams’ “body genres” framework helps interpret why *Facing Mirrors* leans into affect (danger, fear, care, alliance) rather than didactic explanation: melodrama makes legibility through **feeling** when direct discourse is constrained [[124]](https://web.colby.edu/sp266/files/2012/09/filmbodies.pdf). In Iran’s censorship ecology, where “passing” tropes can become representational workarounds [[88]](https://transreads.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/2021-12-23_61c4e7e24d00f_Cross-DressingandGenderTrespassing-TheTransgenderMoveasaSiteofAgentialPotentialintheNewIranianCinema.pdf), melodrama can function as a **politics of indirectness**: the film can stage a taboo encounter as ethical transformation.

### (e) Reading through Najmabadi: “professing selves” and the state’s sorting
Najmabadi argues Iranian institutions have been invested in distinguishing acceptable “true” transsexuality from unacceptable homosexuality, and that regulation produces spaces where recognition depends on narratives people tell—selves they profess [[51]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/professing-selves). In *Facing Mirrors*, Eddie’s struggle is precisely to be recognized as a legitimate subject (for passport mobility, for transition trajectory, for social intelligibility) while family seeks to re-route Eddie into marriage [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors). The film becomes an allegory of “professing” under constraint: Eddie must narrate selfhood into being, yet that narration triggers danger.

**What the film adds to global trans cinema:** it displaces the “individual authenticity” trope toward a **two-person ethics**: trans liberation is not depicted as solitary self-realization but as relational transformation across classed womanhood.

---

## 5.2 *Be Like Others* (Tanaz Eshaghian, 2008, documentary): clinic time, diagnosis as narrative genre, and the documentary ethics of institutional capture

**Core metadata:** Doc Lounge lists *Be Like Others* as a 2008 documentary (74 min) with countries Canada/Iran/UK/USA and describes the film’s context: a fatwa making sex change permissible for “diagnosed transsexuals,” while homosexuality is treated as sinful/illegal; the film visits Dr. Bahram Mir-Jalali’s Tehran clinic and highlights psychiatric permission notes and state ideology distinguishing transsexuality from homosexuality [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others). Cinema Politica similarly frames the film around legality of sex-change operations and the death-penalty context for homosexuality, and it emphasizes the diagnostic-surgical pathway [[53]](https://www.cinemapolitica.org/film/be-others/). An interview with Eshaghian describes her access strategy—finding characters in a surgeon’s waiting room—and explains the title as a desire to “live like everybody else,” to be free from stigmatizing labels [[69]](https://www.vulture.com/2008/01/be_like_others_director_tanaz.html).

### (a) The clinic as cinematic apparatus (and as state interface)
The film’s dramatic center is not romance or family melodrama but **the clinic**, a space where bodies are sorted, narrated, and certified. The Transgender Studies Reader framework on medicalization—sexology, diagnosis, and the production of categories—provides the conceptual base: transness is made legible by being placed into institutional schemas, producing “normal/pathological” distinctions and access criteria [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php). In Iran, this intersects with jurisprudential and administrative logics: the state can recognize transness while criminalizing homosexuality, producing incentives to narrate desire and embodiment in specific ways [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others), [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9745420/).

### (b) Documentary “voice” vs extraction: Nichols, Bruzzi, Renov
Nichols’ classic essay “The Voice of Documentary” offers a way to analyze how documentaries speak—how they organize claims, authority, and address [[108]](https://www.jstor.org/stable/i370747). In *Be Like Others*, the documentary voice is triangulated among (i) subjects’ self-explanations, (ii) medical authorities, and (iii) the film’s framing of legality and danger. Nichols’ book framing also highlights that ethical issues are central to documentary practice [[107]](https://iupress.org/9780253026859/introduction-to-documentary-third-edition/), especially when filming vulnerable subjects.

Bruzzi’s performance-centered understanding of documentary (as framed on the Routledge page) is useful here: the waiting room and consultation are not purely observed realities; they are **performances within power**—subjects perform intelligibility to clinicians, and clinicians perform authority [[109]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9780203967386/new-documentary-stella-bruzzi). Renov’s account of documentary becoming a means to examine/construct selfhood under oppression helps interpret why confession-like scenes are not merely informational; they are identity-work under constraint [[111]](https://www.upress.umn.edu/9780816634415/subject-of-documentary/).

### (c) “Be like others”: transnormativity, respectability, and survival
Eshaghian reports her subjects wanted to be “like everybody else” and rejects labels used against them (degenerate, pervert, etc.) [[69]](https://www.vulture.com/2008/01/be_like_others_director_tanaz.html). A trans-studies reading can treat this desire ambivalently. On one hand, it is a survival wish: to be safe, employable, marriageable, and not hunted. On the other, it can be read as proximity to what later scholarship calls “transnormativity” (not directly sourced in this dataset), i.e., an aspiration to become legible by dominant norms. Spade’s critique of recognition politics warns that “inclusion” strategies can leave structural harms intact [[26]](https://www.deanspade.net/books/normal-life/). The film thus invites a difficult question: is medical transition here an emancipatory path, a coerced compromise, or both?

Doc Lounge explicitly states some same-sex-attracted young people pursue surgery as the only legally open path to a “decent life” [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others). This is where Najmabadi’s argument is essential: Iranian systems distinguish acceptable “true transsexual” from unacceptable “true homosexual,” and selves are “professed” to fit categories [[51]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/professing-selves). *Be Like Others* can be read as documenting how a state’s categorical logic reorganizes desire, identity, and narrative.

### (d) The danger of global spectatorship: “supersaturation” of images of queer/trans Iran
The Hypatia article on Iranian cinema argues that censorship and representational strategies contributed to a “supersaturation” of images of queer/trans Iran in global imaginaries [[88]](https://transreads.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/2021-12-23_61c4e7e24d00f_Cross-DressingandGenderTrespassing-TheTransgenderMoveasaSiteofAgentialPotentialintheNewIranianCinema.pdf). *Be Like Others* sits inside that global consumption field: Western festivals can receive such films as exotic evidence of paradox (“sex change legal, homosexuality punishable”). The UUA screening write-up describes a panel explicitly addressing how Western conceptualizations shape understandings of surgery and sexuality in Iran [[70]](https://www.uua.org/global/news/be-like-others-transgender-life-in-iran). This is a strong cue: the film’s meaning is not only on screen but in how it is **programmed and interpreted**.

**What the film adds:** a documentary of trans life where the central drama is the **institutional production of legitimacy**—and where the camera risks becoming another institution that extracts truth, intensifying the need for documentary ethics.

---

## 5.3 *Blue ID* (Burcu Melekoglu & Vuslat Karan, 2022, Turkey): celebrity, home video, and the administrative object as narrative spine

**Core metadata:** IDFA’s archive describes *Blue ID* (*Mavi kimlik*) as a 2022 Turkish documentary (60 min) about trans actor Rüzgar Erkoçlar, beginning with his first testosterone injection in 2012 and chronicling media attacks, paparazzi pressure, and the milestone of exchanging a “pink identity card” for a “blue one” [[148]](https://www.idfa.nl/en/film/7cd2b58a-edfb-46b8-b986-a8db9a35c3ae/blue-id). The IDFA Bertha Fund report states the film premiered at IDFA 2022 and won the NPO Audience Award; it also describes how the project’s subject was outed by press six months into filming, shifting the film into a confrontation with homophobia/transphobia, and notes the film had not yet premiered in Turkey and faced distribution obstacles [[6]](https://publications.idfa.nl/idfa-bertha-fund-activity-report-2023/in-the-spotlight-burcu-melekoglu-and-vuslat-karan-blue-id). A trans-focused review emphasizes that the film balances hardship with “trans joy” and depicts milestones in a grounded way [[149]](https://transmagazine.nl/blue-id-documentary/).

### (a) The ID card as cinematic MacGuffin—and as Spade’s administrative violence
The pink-to-blue ID exchange is not mere symbolism; it is administrative materiality. In Spade’s terms, the administrative system distributes life chances (employment, safety, mobility) and can be violent even when it offers “recognition” [[26]](https://www.deanspade.net/books/normal-life/). *Blue ID* makes this visible by turning the ID into a narrative hinge: bureaucratic color becomes an aesthetic and existential marker.

### (b) Visibility as scandal: Halberstam’s paradox of being seen
IDFA’s description underscores the film’s central conflict: Turkey makes Erkoçlar front-page news precisely because he was already publicly known as a woman actor [[148]](https://www.idfa.nl/en/film/7cd2b58a-edfb-46b8-b986-a8db9a35c3ae/blue-id). Halberstam’s paradox of visibility/passing is directly activated: public recognition produces forced legibility (“the trans celebrity”) and thus intensified exposure [[27]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003206255-23/transgender-look-jack-halberstam). The “outing” event described in the Bertha Fund report is a rupture in control over temporality: the transition’s timeline is seized by media, reorganizing past/present/future and turning private becoming into public event [[6]](https://publications.idfa.nl/idfa-bertha-fund-activity-report-2023/in-the-spotlight-burcu-melekoglu-and-vuslat-karan-blue-id), [[27]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003206255-23/transgender-look-jack-halberstam).

### (c) Form: “home movie-esque” and phone video—intimacy as counter-public
IDFA notes the film uses “home movie-esque observations and self-assured phone videos” [[148]](https://www.idfa.nl/en/film/7cd2b58a-edfb-46b8-b986-a8db9a35c3ae/blue-id). This matters for film theory: it shifts the film’s address away from institutional voice and toward a self-archived, self-narrated mode. Renov’s framework of documentary as constructing selfhood and autobiographical practice under oppression is relevant: the self becomes the documentary’s site of evidence and agency rather than an object of scientific scrutiny [[111]](https://www.upress.umn.edu/9780816634415/subject-of-documentary/).

At the same time, Bruzzi’s performance account helps avoid naïve realism: phone video is still performance, but potentially performance with greater authorial control than tabloid photography [[109]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/books/mono/10.4324/9780203967386/new-documentary-stella-bruzzi). The film thus stages a battle between **two apparatuses of visibility**: the celebrity media apparatus and the intimate self-recording apparatus.

### (d) Voice and gender: Silverman’s acoustic regime (what changes when a voice “passes”?)
While *Blue ID* is not described in the sources as centrally about voice training, the film’s media environment (interviews, rants, phone videos) makes it a rich object for Silverman’s thesis that cinema’s sound regime produces gendered normativity [[41]](https://monoskop.org/images/b/b4/Silverman_Kaja_The_Acoustic_Mirror_The_Female_Voice_in_Psychoanalysis_and_Cinema_1988.pdf). In celebrity contexts, voice becomes part of public proof: the “right” timbre can be read as authentication or scandal. *Blue ID* therefore invites analysis of how **speech** becomes a site of surveillance and how self-recorded video can reframe voice from object-of-hearing to agent-of-address.

### (e) Circulation politics: why international festivals matter
The Bertha Fund report explicitly notes the film had not premiered in Turkey, details distribution complications, and contextualizes roadblocks within a broader climate of censorship and political pressure (including the cancellation of a festival edition amid censorship controversy involving another documentary) [[6]](https://publications.idfa.nl/idfa-bertha-fund-activity-report-2023/in-the-spotlight-burcu-melekoglu-and-vuslat-karan-blue-id). Together with contemporary reports of bans on LGBTQ film exhibition in Istanbul [[92]](https://www.voanews.com/a/7857598.html), [[93]](https://www.theguardian.com/film/2024/nov/07/turkish-film-festival-daniel-craig-queer-censorship), this indicates that the “public” for Turkish trans cinema is often **constructed abroad**—a condition that shapes not just distribution but also style, explanatory framing, and the ethics of making trans lives legible to external audiences.

**What the film adds:** a model of trans cinema where the central stakes are not only transition but **media capture**, celebrity, and the administrative artifact—told through intimate, self-authored image practices.

---

## 5.4 *Upon the Shadow* (*Au-delà de l’ombre / في الظل*) (Nada Mezni Hafaiedh, 2016/2017, Tunisia): domestic space, precarity, and the festival as exposure machine

**Core metadata:** Thessaloniki Documentary Festival’s official post announces the world premiere of *Upon the Shadow / Au-delà de l’ombre* (Tunisia, 2016, 80’) in a “Human Rights” frame; it describes Amina Sboui housing LGBT friends rejected by family/society and explicitly names **Sandra** as a trans participant [[167]](https://www.facebook.com/filmfestival.gr/posts/tdf19-world-premiereupon-the-shadow-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisia-2017-80-human-ri/1810334835658981/). Apple TV lists the film as 2017, Arabic, 1h20m, Tunisia, directed by Nada Mezni Hafaiedh, with cast including Sandra Neifer [[166]](https://tv.apple.com/us/movie/upon-the-shadow/umc.cmc.2ph0x2ohl48nf5clbjl8vzia2). Carthage Film Festival’s official page documents that *Au-delà de l’ombre* won a **Bronze Tanit** [[170]](https://www.facebook.com/JCC.TN/posts/tanit-de-bronze-%C3%A0-au-del%C3%A0-de-lombre-de-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisie-karim-kamoun/1972660296325857/). Raseef22 documents that Sandra was later bullied and received death threats after appearing on the Carthage red carpet connected to the documentary [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual).

### (a) “Home” as counter-institution—and as a fragile shelter
Unlike *Be Like Others* (clinic) or *Facing Mirrors* (road/police/family), *Upon the Shadow* centers a **household micro-collective**: a domestic space functioning as refuge from family expulsion and social violence. Trans studies emphasizes how vulnerability is distributed by class and institutional exposure (education, work, housing, policing) [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php). The house in this film is a cinematic attempt at “world-making” against those distributions: a fragile, improvised infrastructure of care.

### (b) Documentary ethics becomes literal: the red carpet backlash
Raseef22’s reporting makes the film’s ethical stakes painfully concrete: Sandra’s visibility at the festival produced harassment and death threats; she reported fear for her life and lack of state protection [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual). This underscores Nichols’ insistence that ethical issues are central to documentary filmmaking [[107]](https://iupress.org/9780253026859/introduction-to-documentary-third-edition/). Here, the documentary apparatus extends beyond the camera into festival publicity, press photos, and social media circulation. The film is not only a representation of precarity; it becomes a **generator of risk**.

### (c) The politics of naming and category: “transvestite” as a sign of translation friction
The Thessaloniki post describes Sandra as a “transvestite” [[167]](https://www.facebook.com/filmfestival.gr/posts/tdf19-world-premiereupon-the-shadow-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisia-2017-80-human-ri/1810334835658981/). This is analytically important, not as a “mistake” to correct from outside, but as evidence of the **translation problem** that TSQ identifies as central: how gender concepts travel across linguistic communities and institutional vocabularies [[55]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/tsq-transgender-studies-quarterly). In Tunisia, as human-rights sources indicate, legal recognition is constrained and trans identity may be pathologized in court reasoning [[103]](https://www.humandignitytrust.org/country-profile/Tunisia/). Category friction (transgender/transsexual/transvestite) becomes part of the film’s social reality: the available words are themselves shaped by law, medicine, stigma, and NGO discourse.

### (d) Haptic visuality and the politics of texture (Marks)
A strong way to read *Upon the Shadow* is through Marks’ “haptic visuality”: images that engage the viewer bodily and convey cultural experience and memory through sensory registers rather than purely explanatory speech [[133]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/the-skin-of-the-film). A domestic-space documentary—where lived survival is often registered in surfaces, clothing, cramped rooms, and everyday gestures—lends itself to haptic analysis: the film can create an ethics of proximity without reducing subjects to “case studies.”

**What the film adds:** a North African trans/queer documentary where the central drama is not transition-as-procedure but **collective survival**, and where festival circulation itself becomes a site of transphobic violence.

---

### 6) Comparative synthesis: recurring patterns and meaningful differences across the corpus

#### 6.1 Institution-heavy storytelling: the clinic, the court, the ID, the passport
Across Iran (clinic permissions, fatwa legality, passports) [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others), [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9745420/), [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors) and Turkey (legal criteria; ID card color; ECHR case) [[91]](https://www.amnesty.org/es/wp-content/uploads/2021/05/EUR4412052015ENGLISH.pdf), [[148]](https://www.idfa.nl/en/film/7cd2b58a-edfb-46b8-b986-a8db9a35c3ae/blue-id), the recurring cinematic pattern is **transition as institutionally narrated**. This supports Prosser’s “sex change is a plot” proposition: not because transition is reducible to plot, but because institutions demand narrative sequences (diagnosis → approval → document → surgery → recognition) [[28]](https://cup.columbia.edu/book/second-skins/9780231533805/).

#### 6.2 Family melodrama as a “public pedagogy” under taboo
In *Facing Mirrors*, family pressure (marriage) and classed gender norms structure danger and alliance [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors). In Israel/Palestine contexts, *Melting Away* is explicitly structured as family expulsion and return, using illness and care as reconciliation devices [[81]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline37/melting-away). The director describes the film as “for parents,” aiming to shift norms of unconditional love [[82]](https://epgn.com/2012/03/29/18042961-warming-up-to-melting-away-israeli-film-festival-to-screen-lgbt-title/). Williams’ melodrama framework helps clarify why these films lean on affective excess: melodrama is a genre for making moral legibility when direct political speech may be constrained [[124]](https://web.colby.edu/sp266/files/2012/09/filmbodies.pdf).

#### 6.3 Documentary exposure and the ethics of “voice”
Nichols’ “voice” concept becomes crucial across *Be Like Others* (voice split between subjects and medical authority) [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others), [[108]](https://www.jstor.org/stable/i370747) and *Upon the Shadow* (voice as risky visibility) [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual), [[167]](https://www.facebook.com/filmfestival.gr/posts/tdf19-world-premiereupon-the-shadow-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisia-2017-80-human-ri/1810334835658981/). The Tunisian backlash demonstrates that documentary voice is not just stylistic; it is a social event.

#### 6.4 Voice as gendered discipline: from clinic confession to street-night testimony
Silverman’s acoustic theory suggests analyzing not only what is shown but how gender is produced through sound regimes and speech extraction [[41]](https://monoskop.org/images/b/b4/Silverman_Kaja_The_Acoustic_Mirror_The_Female_Voice_in_Psychoanalysis_and_Cinema_1988.pdf). This is especially suggestive for:
- **street-night documentaries** like *The Belle from Gaza*, where subjects narrate dangers, faith, family rupture, and concealment [[151]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/2024/interview-with-yolande-zauberman-searching-for-the-legendary-belle-from-gaza/), and  
- **performer-centered narratives** like *Melting Away*, where Anna’s stage performance and later nursing disguise hinge on vocal and bodily presentation [[81]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline37/melting-away), [[80]](https://doroneran.com/melting-away/).

#### 6.5 Transnational imaginaries: borders, diaspora publics, and festival geopolitics
Even when stories are “local,” their publics are often transnational:
- *Facing Mirrors* is literally oriented toward **Germany** as imagined surgical horizon [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors).  
- *Blue ID* is shaped by the difficulty of domestic premiere and the construction of an international festival public [[6]](https://publications.idfa.nl/idfa-bertha-fund-activity-report-2023/in-the-spotlight-burcu-melekoglu-and-vuslat-karan-blue-id).  
- *The Belle from Gaza* is a French production about Arab trans women in Tel Aviv nightlife, premiering in Cannes Special Screenings and circulating widely via Pyramide’s sales network [[150]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/f/la-belle-de-gaza/), [[154]](http://inter.pyramidefilms.com/pyramide-international-catalogue-t/the-belle-from-gaza.html).  
This pattern resonates with transnational approaches to gender reassignment imaginaries (as indicated by scholarship on transnational reassignment economies) [[34]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/mobile-subjects), even where the film is not explicitly about “medical tourism.”

#### 6.6 Community archive as aesthetics and politics (Lebanon)
*Treat Me Like Your Mother* (IDFA 2025) foregrounds **photo archives** and multi-decade Beirut trans social worlds, framing itself as built against “arguably the first photographic archive of trans women in Lebanon” and recounting a history from state-funded surgery in 1997 to vanished safe spaces [[1]](https://festival.idfa.nl/en/film/a6aa4102-ea72-4f6e-a7f0-21d17dcfd526/treat-me-like-your-mother/). Marks’ haptic visuality is again relevant: archives can be tactile—creases, prints, domestic video—and can produce a counter-history that resists both state erasure and NGO statistical reduction [[133]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/the-skin-of-the-film), [[1]](https://festival.idfa.nl/en/film/a6aa4102-ea72-4f6e-a7f0-21d17dcfd526/treat-me-like-your-mother/). The film’s scrapbook form also aligns with documentary traditions that foreground personal archive and first-person entanglement (Renov’s selfhood-construction emphasis) [[111]](https://www.upress.umn.edu/9780816634415/subject-of-documentary/).

---

### 7) Israel/Palestine-focused note: *The Belle from Gaza* as nocturnal quest, geopolitics, and the ethics of representing precarious lives

Because the film is recent and well-documented in official festival materials, it is worth a focused (if shorter) analytic pass.

**Core metadata:** Official Cannes page lists *The Belle from Gaza* as Special Screenings 2024; duration 76 min; production year 2023; country France; with distribution and sales by Pyramide [[150]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/f/la-belle-de-gaza/). Cannes interview describes Hatnufa Street (trans sex work site) and the director’s search for the rumored “Belle,” and notes safety practices (veil/hijab to avoid recognition) [[151]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/2024/interview-with-yolande-zauberman-searching-for-the-legendary-belle-from-gaza/). Pyramide catalog provides languages, credits, and extensive festival itinerary, including TLVFest where it received an Honorable Mention in Documentary Competition [[154]](http://inter.pyramidefilms.com/pyramide-international-catalogue-t/the-belle-from-gaza.html).

**Key theoretical tensions:**
- **Visibility vs safety:** the film thematizes concealment and danger directly (veil/hijab; legal status risks) [[151]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/2024/interview-with-yolande-zauberman-searching-for-the-legendary-belle-from-gaza/). This is Halberstam’s paradox in a different register: “to be seen” is simultaneously evidence and threat [[27]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003206255-23/transgender-look-jack-halberstam).  
- **Documentary ethics:** Nichols’ ethics centrality is amplified when subjects are precarious migrants/sex workers in a heavily policed geopolitical space [[107]](https://iupress.org/9780253026859/introduction-to-documentary-third-edition/), [[151]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/2024/interview-with-yolande-zauberman-searching-for-the-legendary-belle-from-gaza/).  
- **Festival geopolitics:** exhibition contexts in Israel/Palestine are overlaid by “pinkwashing” contestation and boycott calls; advocacy groups explicitly frame queer film circulation as political action and contest state-linked festival funding [[99]](https://queercinemaforpalestine.org/), [[100]](https://queercinemaforpalestine.org/faqs/). A film like *The Belle from Gaza* will therefore be read not only as trans testimony but as a position within those conflicts.

---

### 8) Debates, pitfalls, and open problems (what a rigorous paper must not romanticize)

1) **Equating surgery legality with trans liberation**  
Iran’s legality and partial subsidization can coexist with discrimination, honor violence, and coercive pressures shaped by the criminalization of homosexuality and by administrative sorting [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9745420/), [[17]](http://c4991.cloudnet.se/films/be-like-others). Films that foreground surgery access must be read against those structural conditions (Najmabadi; Spade) [[51]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/professing-selves), [[26]](https://www.deanspade.net/books/normal-life/).

2) **Documentary as protection vs documentary as exposure**  
*Upon the Shadow* demonstrates that the festival/publicity apparatus can become a violence vector [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual). This requires a research stance that treats documentary circulation as part of the film’s ethical field (Nichols; Renov) [[107]](https://iupress.org/9780253026859/introduction-to-documentary-third-edition/), [[111]](https://www.upress.umn.edu/9780816634415/subject-of-documentary/).

3) **Translation and category discipline**  
Terms like “transsexual,” “transvestite,” and “gender identity disorder” appear across materials [[167]](https://www.facebook.com/filmfestival.gr/posts/tdf19-world-premiereupon-the-shadow-nada-mezni-hafaiedh-tunisia-2017-80-human-ri/1810334835658981/), [[183]](https://www.voanews.com/a/transgender-operations-stall-egypt/3367741.html). TSQ frames translation across linguistic communities as a central problem [[55]](https://www.dukeupress.edu/tsq-transgender-studies-quarterly). Scholarly writing must neither impose Anglophone categories as universal nor pretend local labels are neutral; the labels are produced under law, medicine, religion, and media.

4) **Censorship is not only state prohibition**  
Turkey’s recent exhibition bans demonstrate formal state power [[92]](https://www.voanews.com/a/7857598.html), [[93]](https://www.theguardian.com/film/2024/nov/07/turkish-film-festival-daniel-craig-queer-censorship), but Iran’s case also shows non-state actor violence against theaters over gender transgression [[88]](https://transreads.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/2021-12-23_61c4e7e24d00f_Cross-DressingandGenderTrespassing-TheTransgenderMoveasaSiteofAgentialPotentialintheNewIranianCinema.pdf). Lebanon’s formal pre-censorship is a legal infrastructure that can condition content via cuts and prohibitions [[95]](https://lb.boell.org/sites/default/files/2010._censorship_in_lebanon-_law_practice_en.pdf). Any account of “MENA censorship” must include both formal and informal coercions.

---

### 9) Concluding synthesis: what MENA trans-themed cinema contributes to trans/film theory

- It forces film theory to take **administrative objects** (ID cards, passports, court approvals) as central cinematic agents rather than props—aligning with trans studies’ focus on classification regimes [[25]](https://routledgetextbooks.com/textbooks/_author/stryker-9780415517737/primer.php), [[26]](https://www.deanspade.net/books/normal-life/).  
- It reframes the politics of the gaze: the ethical question is not only “who looks at whom?” (Mulvey) [[105]](https://horrorlex.com/results/visual-pleasure-and-narrative-cinema/Mulvey,%20Laura/686529383692b965e8f9e585), but also “what happens when looking becomes evidence that triggers policing, outing, or family expulsion?” (Halberstam; Tunisia backlash) [[27]](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.4324/9781003206255-23/transgender-look-jack-halberstam), [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual).  
- It expands documentary ethics from consent-in-the-moment to **risk-across-circulation**: festivals, press photos, and social media become extensions of the documentary apparatus [[107]](https://iupress.org/9780253026859/introduction-to-documentary-third-edition/), [[160]](https://raseef22.net/english/article/1076278-sandra-attacked-at-carthage-film-festival-for-being-transsexual).  
- It highlights voice as a decisive site of gendering and extraction (Silverman), especially in clinics, interviews, performance spaces, and night-street testimonies [[41]](https://monoskop.org/images/b/b4/Silverman_Kaja_The_Acoustic_Mirror_The_Female_Voice_in_Psychoanalysis_and_Cinema_1988.pdf), [[151]](https://www.festival-cannes.com/en/2024/interview-with-yolande-zauberman-searching-for-the-legendary-belle-from-gaza/).  
- It demonstrates that melodrama and affect (Williams) are not merely conservative forms; they can be **public pedagogy** under censorship and taboo [[124]](https://web.colby.edu/sp266/files/2012/09/filmbodies.pdf), [[52]](https://www.frameline.org/films/frameline36/facing-mirrors), [[82]](https://epgn.com/2012/03/29/18042961-warming-up-to-melting-away-israeli-film-festival-to-screen-lgbt-title/).
